summary: High-risk regulatory changes for food seasoning/snack compliance are widespread. Codex has new contaminant limits (mycotoxins, metals) requiring verification. GCC/GSO introduced comprehensive updates across labelling (general, Halal, GMOs, expiration dates), allergen declarations, and microbiological criteria, all necessitating verification against new GSO standards. Singapore now mandates strict compliance for food additives (only SFA-assessed permitted) and contaminants (within SFA limits), alongside mandatory ingredient/additive labelling. Taiwan expanded mandatory allergen labelling to 11 specific items, including a 10 mg/kg threshold for sulphite. In the United States, key changes include mandatory declaration of nine major food allergens (now including sesame, effective Jan 1, 2023) with specific formats and new preventive controls, prohibition of delisted/prohibited additives, and updated contaminant limits for total aflatoxins (20 ppb action level) and 3-MCPD (1 ppm guidance). Thailand has new prohibitions on certain food products. Lastly, Philippines labelling requirements need manual review due to a data extraction issue.

changes.parameter: Contaminants (e.g., mycotoxins, metals like arsenic, lead, mercury)
changes.newLimit: Verification required via Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995)
changes.notes: The Codex Alimentarius Commission establishes maximum levels (MLs) for contaminants to be legally permitted in food or feed commodities. These levels are detailed in standards like CXS 193-1995.

changes.parameter: Declaration of allergens on labels of prepackaged foodstuffs
changes.notes: Specific requirements for allergen declaration are detailed within the GSO 9:2022 standard.

changes.parameter: General labelling requirements for prepackaged foodstuffs
changes.notes: This technical regulation outlines comprehensive labelling requirements for all prepackaged food products.
Specific labelling requirements for prepackaged foodstuffs must be verified by consulting the full GSO 9:2022 standard.

changes.parameter: General requirements for genetically modified agricultural products
changes.notes: Specific general requirements for non-processed genetically modified agricultural products must be verified by consulting the full GSO 2141:2026 standard. This may include labelling requirements for GMOs.
Specific general requirements for Halal food must be verified by consulting the full GSO 2055-1:2015 standard.
Specific mandatory expiration date requirements for food products must be verified by consulting the full GSO 150-1:2013 standard.
Specific microbiological limits for foodstuffs and food ingredients must be verified by consulting the full GSO 1016:2015 standard.

changes.newLimit: Must be declared on the label
changes.notes: The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame. Sesame was added effective January 1, 2023, by the FASTER Act.
This is a Guidance Level.
This is an Action Level. Specific limits for Brazil nuts, peanuts, and pistachio nuts are also 20 ppb.

changes.newLimit: Establishments must put written procedures in place to control allergen cross-contact and ensure accurate allergen labeling, as per 21 CFR part 117 (CGMP & PC rule).
changes.notes: Part of the 'Current Good Manufacturing Practice, Hazard Analysis, and Risk-Based Preventive Controls for Human Food' rule.

changes.newLimit: Must be declared on the label
changes.notes: Identified as allergenic substances derived from insects.

changes.newLimit: Must be declared in one of two ways: in parentheses after the ingredient name, OR in a 'contains' statement immediately after or next to the ingredient list.
changes.notes: The type of tree nut (e.g., almonds), species of fish (e.g., bass), and species of Crustacean shellfish (e.g., crab) must also be declared.
Required due to potential hypersensitivity reactions in some individuals.

changes.newLimit: Must meet a defined standard for gluten content
changes.notes: The FDA issued a final rule defining 'gluten-free' for food labeling on August 2, 2013, and established compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods bearing the 'gluten-free' claim on August 12, 2020.
Must be declared on food labels when present in food at or above the specified concentration, due to adverse reactions in sensitive individuals.

Specific regulations for additive limits in seasoning, snack food, and processed foods must be verified through official documents published on the government's legal document portal, particularly under the 'Y tế - Sức khỏe' (Health - Healthcare) sector.

This regulation defines food categories, which is fundamental for understanding and applying specific compliance rules for processed foods, including labelling.
Applies to ready-to-eat (RTE) food.

changes.newLimit: Not required by law; should only be used to address unavoidable cross-contact after good manufacturing practices have been incorporated and precautions taken. Must be truthful and not misleading.
